pitch_rationale: The homes at this Spring, TX property display classic Texas suburban hip-roof construction. Shadow lengths cast from eave edges onto the ground are moderate — neither the short stubs of a 4:12 nor the elongated projections of an 8:12+ roof — pointing to a mid-range slope. The ratio of visible roof surface (nadir eave-to-ridge distance) to overall building footprint is consistent with a 6:12 pitch: the ridge lines are clearly defined but occupy a meaningful fraction of the plan width, ruling out very shallow (4:12) or steep (8:12+) slopes. Valley and hip intersection shadows show well-defined but not dramatic depth, further corroborating a 6:12 estimate. The southern Texas climate and architectural style of the neighborhood (multi-plane hip roofs on large suburban homes) are also strongly associated with 6:12 as the dominant pitch in this region.
footprint_rationale: The orange bounding box measures approximately 228 px wide × 338 px tall (48.3 ft × 71.6 ft), giving a raw bounding-box area of ~3,459 sq ft. The subject home displays a complex multi-section hip roof with multiple pyramidal peaks, numerous hip diagonals, and several interior valleys where sections intersect. No prominent gable rakes are visible (hip-dominant construction). A fill factor of 0.84 was applied to account for the hip corners, yielding a footprint of ~2,906 sq ft. Ridge lines consist of 3 horizontal peak segments spanning the upper, middle, and lower sections (~51 ft total). Hip edges account for ~10 diagonal corner runs (~121 ft). Valley lines trace ~5 inward angles between intersecting roof planes (~60 ft). Rake edges are minimal with only 1–2 small gable tips (~14 ft). Eave lines run along the outer lower perimeter (~162 ft). Confidence is moderate (0.52) due to the complex, overlapping roof geometry making precise edge tracing difficult from plan view.
fence_reason: vision roof area 3249 differs from solar 4369 by 25.6% (>20%)
